title: Add refunds endpoint and customer transaction history
summary: Started from 26 raw findings; after verifying each against the diff and collapsing duplicates, 15 survive. Strong cross-persona agreement clustered on three real issues that I kept as high-confidence: the deliberate `card_number` PAN exposure (flagged independently by kiss_zealot, quality_critic, and security_hawk), the silent over-refund/dual-unit coercion (kiss_zealot + quality_critic), the broken `created_at` sort (kiss_zealot + quality_critic), the module-level `refundsStore` singleton (kiss_zealot + quality_critic), the vacuous test assertions (kiss_zealot + quality_critic), and the console.log-vs-pino logging (quality_critic + security_hawk). I merged the three perf findings into two (unbounded findAll/no-pagination, and the N+1 + unindexed refund scan) since they describe the same underlying retrieval design. The two auth findings (missing authn on the routes, and the BOLA/IDOR on the history endpoint) are kept separate because they live in different files and address authentication vs. authorization. Nothing was dropped as an outright false positive — the panel was accurate this round — but I down-graded several 'must' perf/state items to 'should' since this is an in-memory placeholder, and kept genuine PAN/money/auth issues at 'must'. Honest-severity calls: PAN leak, over-refund, and missing auth are 'must'; everything else is 'should' or 'suggestion'.

findings.title: No authentication middleware on POST /refunds or GET /customers/:id/transactions
findings.message: Both new routes are registered with no auth guard. POST /refunds lets any unauthenticated client issue a refund against any transaction id — a direct financial-control failure. Wire an `authenticate` middleware (JWT/API-key) on these routes (or the whole router if the pattern is consistent) before merge. The existing /charge route shares the gap, but adding refund *creation* without auth materially raises the risk.

findings.title: BOLA/IDOR — any caller can read any customer's transaction history
findings.message: `customerId` comes straight from `req.params.id` and is used to return that customer's full transaction and refund history with no check that the requesting principal owns or is authorised for that customer. Anyone who can enumerate customer ids gets amounts, refund totals, gateway references, and card-last4 for every account (OWASP API #1). Add an authorisation check (e.g. assert `req.user.customerId === customerId`, or a scope check for service tokens) before the repo call.

findings.title: card_number cast forwards a full PAN into the HTTP response — PCI hazard
findings.message: `card_number: (tx as unknown as { card_number?: string }).card_number ?? tx.cardLast4` uses a double-cast to punch through TypeScript and surface a field the type system says doesn't exist. Even if the field is currently absent, the intent is to forward a full PAN as a top-level JSON key — a PCI-DSS Req 3 violation the moment any runtime/gateway change populates it. Delete the `card_number` key entirely; `card_last4` (the next line) is the maximum that may be exposed. (The response also mixes snake_case/camelCase vs. other controllers — pick one convention while you're here.)

findings.title: Over-refund silently capped instead of rejected; speculative dollar/cent coercion
findings.message: When `refundAmount > tx.amount` the code guesses a dollar→cent conversion and otherwise silently clamps to `tx.amount`, returning a 201 that claims success. A caller requesting a $1000 refund on a $10 charge gets a $10 refund and no error. There is no client in this diff that sends dollars — this is an unvalidated, untested guess on money-handling code. Remove the coercion block and reject amounts exceeding the transaction with a 422 domain error.

findings.title: amount: z.number().optional() accepts zero, negatives, and fractional cents
findings.message: `z.number()` with no constraints lets 0, -500, and 9.99 reach refund logic that assumed they couldn't happen, compounding the over-refund coercion. Tighten to `z.number().int().positive().optional()` to pin the unit to cents and reject nonsense at the boundary.

findings.title: Returns 200 {} when transaction is not found — should be 404
findings.message: When `refund()` returns null the controller sends `res.status(200).json({})`. An empty 200 tells callers and any retry/idempotency layer that the refund succeeded. Return `res.status(404).json({ error: 'transaction not found' })` instead.

findings.title: Sort key created_at does not exist on Transaction — sort is a silent no-op
findings.message: The comparator is typed `(a: any, b: any)` so the compiler won't flag that `Transaction` uses camelCase `createdAt`, not `created_at`. At runtime every comparison is `undefined < undefined` → 0, so the array is never reordered and the PR's 'newest first' promise is broken. Remove the `any`, sort on `createdAt`, and let the compiler confirm the field.

findings.title: Module-level refundsStore singleton — hidden global state, leaks across tests
findings.message: `refundsStore` is a module-scope `Record`, unlike every other dependency here which is injected via the constructor. Every `RefundsService` instance secretly shares it, so the three `createApp()` calls in the test suite are not isolated — refunds from one test are visible in the next. Model a `RefundsRepository`, construct it in `createApp`, and inject it the way `TransactionRepository` already is.

findings.title: All three test assertions are vacuously true and catch nothing
findings.message: `expect(refund.body).toBeDefined()` (supertest always sets body to at least {}), `expect(refund.status).toBeLessThan(500)` (passes on 200/201/400/404 alike), and `expect(res.body).toBeDefined()` would all stay green if the handlers returned garbage. Assert `status === 201` and the shape of `refund_id`/`transaction_id`/`amount`/`status` on the happy path; assert `404` on the missing-transaction path; assert `transactions` is an array on history. Add over-refund-rejection and partial-refund cases.

findings.title: console.log bypasses the pino logger (and its redaction) for financial fields
findings.message: RefundsService lines 23 and 50, plus customerHistoryController line 16, use `console.log` and concatenate transaction ids / customer ids / amounts into the message string, bypassing the project's pino logger and its redaction allowlist. Depending on log collection this writes PCI-adjacent data to raw stdout. Use structured fields via the request-scoped logger: `req.log.info({ customerId }, 'history requested')`, `logger.warn({ transactionId }, 'refund: transaction not found')`.

findings.title: Unbounded findAll()-then-filter with no pagination on the history endpoint
findings.message: The handler calls `findAll()` (now a first-class repository capability) to load every transaction ever stored, filters by customerId in a JS loop, and returns the whole set with no limit/offset/cursor. On seed data this is invisible; against a real DB it's an O(total_rows) full scan plus an unbounded response. Replace `findAll()` with a filtered, paginated `findByCustomerId(id, { limit, cursor })` (default limit 50, max 200) backed by `WHERE customer_id = $1 ... LIMIT`, and keep `findAll()` out of request-handling paths.

findings.title: N+1 refund lookup over an unindexed store — O(customer_txns × total_refunds)
findings.message: `enrichTransactionWithRefunds` is awaited once per transaction in the history loop, and each call runs `listRefundsForTransaction`, which scans the entire `refundsStore` (O(total refunds)) with no index. Combined cost per request is O(N_txns × M_refunds). Index the store by transactionId (`Record<transactionId, RefundResult[]>`) for O(1) lookup, and fetch refunds for all transaction ids in a single batched call outside the loop instead of N scans.

findings.title: == used for customerId comparison instead of ===
findings.message: `tx.customerId == customerId` uses loose equality against the project's `eqeqeq` convention. Both operands are strings so behaviour is unchanged today, but use `===` for consistency.

findings.title: historyQuerySchema is defined but never imported or used
findings.message: Dead export — nothing references `historyQuerySchema`, and the history controller reads `req.params.id` directly without validating it. Either wire it into real validation or delete it.

findings.title: _reason parameter flows through schema and service then is discarded
findings.message: `reason` is accepted by the Zod schema, passed through the controller and service, then prefixed `_reason` and never stored, logged, or returned. Either persist it on the refund record or drop it from the schema and signature until there's a real use.
